perturbation.original_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information and Exclusions. (a) “Confidential Information” means nonpublic information that a party to this Agreement (“Disclosing Party”) designates as being confidential to the partythat receives such information (“Receiving Party”) or which, under thecircumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential by theReceiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition of Confidential Information and Exclusions. (a) “Confidential Information” means any information disclosed under this agreement, regardless of whether it is marked confidential or not. The designation of information as confidential is at the sole discretion of the Disclosing Party, but all information shared is to be treated as confidential under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include any information disclosed, irrespective of whether it is explicitly designated as confidential. This contradicts Section 1(b), which outlines specific exclusions to what is considered Confidential Information. The original text specifies how it ought to be treated and the changed text is including regardless of anything
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Confidential Information shall not include any information, however designated, that: (i) is or subsequently becomes publicly available without Receiving Party’s breach of any obligation owed Disclosing Party; (ii) became known to Receiving Party prior to Disclosing Party’s disclosure of such information to Receiving Party pursuant to the terms of thisAgreement; (iii) became known to Receiving Party from a source other than Disclosing Party other than by the breach of an obligation of confidentiality owed toDisclosing Party; (iv) is independently developed by Receiving Party; or(v) constitutes Feedback (as defined in Section 5 of this Agreement).
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include any information disclosed, regardless of whether it is explicitly designated as confidential. This directly contradicts Section 1(b), which lists specific exclusions to what is considered Confidential Information. The original text allows for certain information to be excluded, whereas the new text dictates all information is confidential, regardless. Therefore a strong contradiction is introduced. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for ten (10) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for five (5) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'ten (10) years' to 'five (5) years' creates a contradiction. If Section 4(j) refers back to this specific term length for the survival of obligations, it now conflicts with the stated duration in Section 2(a)(i).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (j) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreementrelating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning ConfidentialInformation disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any suchtermination.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'ten (10) years' to 'five (5) years' in the modified text directly contradicts the terms of survival outlined in section 4(j). Section 4(j) states that obligations concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the *term* of the agreement survive termination. The term length, now five years in the changed text, directly impacts the duration these obligations survive. The original duration of ten years now breaks compatibility with the survival period implicitly tied to the agreement's term, hence a clear in-text contradiction exists as defined by the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction perturbation definition, as there's an inconsistency regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: (d) Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way tohelp Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information andprevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party within 30 days of discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party as is reasonably convenient to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information andprevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' and 'in every reasonable way' to 'as is reasonably convenient' introduces ambiguity. The initial text makes it clear that a quick notification is needed along with a stronger cooperation. This change contradicts the rights to remedies stated in Section 3(a) that expects immediate action.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Rights and Remedies (a) Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information orany other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees andconsultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way tohelp Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information andprevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notification period from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' and the cooperation level from 'in every reasonable way' to 'as is reasonably convenient'. This directly contradicts the original wording and the rights to remedies stated in the 'Contradicted Text' section. The 'Contradicted Text' section also requires immediate notification and full cooperation, which the changed text weakens. This leads to a clear and direct conflict, uncertainty, and potential unenforceability, since there are different obligations, and satisfies the conditions for ambiguity and inconsistency in the definitions provided.

perturbation.original_text: (b) In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms ofthis Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may only be used bythe Receiving Party for evaluation and providing Feedback (as defined inSection 5 of this Agreement) to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) In the event that the Disclosing Party provides any computer software and/or hardware to the Receiving Party as Confidential Information under the terms ofthis Agreement, such computer software and/or hardware may be used by the Receiving Party for any purpose, including but not limited to evaluation, providing Feedback (as defined inSection 5 of this Agreement) to the Disclosing Party, or commercial exploitation.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'may only be used for evaluation and providing Feedback' to 'may be used for any purpose, including ... commercial exploitation,' Receiving Party is now free to use Disclosing Party’s software/hardware for commercial gain. This contradicts Section 4(a) which states that Disclosing Party doesn't grant any express rights under any patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secret information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party. By disclosing Confidential Information to Receiving Party, DisclosingParty does not grant any express or implied right to Receiving Party to or underany patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secret information except asotherwise provided herein. Disclosing Party reserves without prejudice theability to protect its rights under any such patents, copyrights, trademarks, ortrade secrets except as otherwise provided herein.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited the Receiving Party's use of Disclosing Party's software/hardware to evaluation and feedback. The changed text allows for any purpose, including commercial exploitation. This directly contradicts Section 4(a) which states that the Disclosing Party does not grant any rights under patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secrets, except as otherwise provided in the agreement. The change allows commercial exploitation, which implies granting rights, thus contradicting the original limitation.

perturbation.original_text: (g) This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party’s respective successors and lawful assigns; provided, however, that neither partymay assign this Agreement (whether by operation of law, sale of securities orassets, merger or otherwise), in whole or in part, without the prior writtenapproval of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party’s respective successors and lawful assigns; provided, however, that either partymay assign this Agreement (whether by operation of law, sale of securities orassets, merger or otherwise), in whole or in part, with notification to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'prior written approval' to 'notification,' this significantly reduces the control the non-assigning party has over who becomes a party to the agreement. This conflicts with rights stated in section 4(i), which mentions that if Confidential Information is disclosed to another party, both parties are responsible for any infringement consequences.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (i) If any Confidential Information is disclosed to any other party, both the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall be deemed to be equallyresponsible for the consequences of such disclosure, including, but not restrictedto, liability for patent or copyright infringement of any other party’s code thusdisclosed, and/or failure to obtain the necessary permission to use the code orproprietary information contained therein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the assignment clause, replacing 'prior written approval' with 'notification.' This creates a direct conflict with section 4(i), which holds both the disclosing and receiving parties equally responsible for the consequences of disclosing confidential information to 'any other party.' The original text's requirement for prior written approval provided a mechanism to control the assignment and thus mitigate the risks associated with confidential information disclosure. By removing this control, the changed text weakens the protections afforded by section 4(i) and introduces a contradiction regarding the level of control and responsibility for confidential information. The change makes it possible to assign the agreement with mere notification, potentially to an entity that the other party would not have approved, thereby increasing the risk of liability under section 4(i).

perturbation.original_text: 5. Suggestions and Feedback The Receiving Party may from time to time provide suggestions, comments or other feedback (“Feedback”) to the Disclosing Party with respect to ConfidentialInformation provided originally by the Disclosing Party. Both parties agree that allFeedback is and shall be given entirely voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated asconfidential by the Disclosing Party, shall not, absent a separate written agreement,create any confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Suggestions and Feedback The Receiving Party may from time to time provide suggestions, comments or other feedback (“Feedback”) to the Disclosing Party with respect to ConfidentialInformation provided originally by the Disclosing Party. All Feedback is and shall be considered Confidential Information regardless of whether is it given voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated asconfidential by the Disclosing Party, shall create confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original definition by asserting that all feedback is confidential, regardless of its voluntary nature, and creating a confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party. The original states that Feedback shall not, absent a separate written agreement, create any confidentiality obligation. This is contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Suggestions and Feedback The Receiving Party may from time to time provide suggestions, comments or other feedback (“Feedback”) to the Disclosing Party with respect to ConfidentialInformation provided originally by the Disclosing Party. All Feedback is and shall be considered Confidential Information regardless of whether is it given voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated asconfidential by the Disclosing Party, shall create confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party. Furthermore, except asotherwise provided herein or in a separate subsequent written agreement between theparties, the Receiving Party shall be free to use, disclose, reproduce, license orotherwise distribute, and exploit the Feedback provided to it as it sees fit, entirelywithout obligation or restriction of any kind on account of intellectual property rightsor otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 5 explicitly states that "All Feedback is and shall be considered Confidential Information regardless of whether is it given voluntarily. Feedback, even if designated as confidential by the Disclosing Party, shall create confidentiality obligation for the Receiving Party." The contradicted text (which is actually the same text) continues by saying "Furthermore, except as otherwise provided herein or in a separate subsequent written agreement between the parties, the Receiving Party shall be free to use, disclose, reproduce, license or otherwise distribute, and exploit the Feedback provided to it as it sees fit, entirely without obligation or restriction of any kind on account of intellectual property rights or otherwise." These statements are directly contradictory. The first part of the changed text (also the contradicted text) states that feedback creates a confidentiality obligation, while the second part states that the receiving party is free to use the feedback without any obligation or restriction. This is a clear and direct conflict within the document, thus a strong in-text contradiction exists.
